questions.question: Explain the difference between horizontal and vertical scalability in distributed systems. Which is more commonly preferred and why?
keyPoints: Horizontal scalability: adding more machines/nodes to the system
Vertical scalability: adding more resources (CPU, RAM, storage) to existing machines
Horizontal is preferred in distributed systems because it provides linear scaling and fault tolerance
Vertical has hardware limits and creates single points of failure
Examples: Adding new servers (horizontal) vs upgrading a server (vertical)

questions.question: Define middleware in distributed systems and list four essential services it provides to application developers.
keyPoints: Middleware is a software layer between OS and applications
Provides transparency by hiding distribution complexity
Essential services: Communication (message passing, RPC), Transaction management
Naming/Directory services for location transparency
Security services (authentication, authorization)
Data representation standardization for heterogeneous systems

questions.question: Describe three types of transparency in distributed systems with practical examples for each.
keyPoints: Access transparency: Hide differences in data representation (e.g., different file formats)
Location transparency: Hide where resources are located (e.g., URL in web)
Migration transparency: Allow resource movement (e.g., live VM migration)
Replication transparency: Hide that multiple copies exist (e.g., DNS replication)
Concurrency transparency: Hide parallel access (e.g., shared calendars)
Failure transparency: Hide failures from users (e.g., automatic failover)

questions.question: Why is the absence of a global clock considered both a challenge and a characteristic of distributed systems?
keyPoints: No single synchronized time across all nodes
Events ordering becomes difficult (happened-before relation)
Requires logical clocks (Lamport, Vector) for ordering
Makes coordination and consistency harder to achieve
Also enables true distribution - avoids single point of time synchronization
Different nodes may have slightly different notions of time

questions.question: Explain the CAP theorem and justify why distributed systems must choose between consistency and availability during network partitions.
keyPoints: CAP: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance - can guarantee only two
Network partitions are inevitable in distributed systems (geography, network issues)
During partition: must choose between C and A
Choose Consistency: reject some requests to maintain data agreement
Choose Availability: serve requests but may return stale data
Modern systems typically choose AP and use eventual consistency

questions.question: Discuss the major challenges in designing distributed systems. For each challenge, explain why it is unique or more severe in distributed systems compared to centralized systems.
keyPoints: Heterogeneity: Different hardware, OS, networks require standardization
Openness: Extensibility needs well-defined interfaces and standards
Security: More attack surfaces due to network exposure and multiple nodes
Scalability: Must handle growth in users, data, geographical spread
Failure handling: Partial failures are unique - some nodes work while others fail
Concurrency: Multiple independent processes accessing shared resources without global lock
Transparency: Hard to achieve all transparency types simultaneously
Each challenge interacts with others creating complex trade-offs

questions.question: Compare and contrast client-server architecture with peer-to-peer architecture in distributed systems. Under what circumstances would you choose one over the other?
keyPoints: Client-server: Centralized server provides services, clients request; simple but single point of failure
Peer-to-peer: All nodes are equal, can be both client and server; decentralized, scalable
Client-server advantages: Easier to secure, manage, maintain consistency
Client-server disadvantages: Scalability limits, single point of failure
P2P advantages: No bottleneck, resilient to failures, scales organically
P2P disadvantages: Harder to ensure consistency, security challenges
Choose client-server: When strong consistency needed, simpler management
Choose P2P: Large-scale content sharing, streaming, blockchain applications

questions.question: Explain what is meant by partial failures in distributed systems. Why are they particularly difficult to handle, and what strategies can be employed to manage them?
keyPoints: Partial failure: Some components fail while others continue operating
Different from centralized systems where failure is all-or-nothing
Hard to detect: Network partitions may make failed nodes appear alive
Hard to diagnose: Failure may be in network, not nodes
Strategies: Timeouts and retries, Heartbeat protocols to detect failures
Quorum-based decisions for consistency despite failures
State machine replication for fault tolerance
Circuit breakers to prevent cascade failures
Trade-offs: Complexity vs reliability, consistency vs availability
